Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule because I was constantly dealing with financial stress and anxiety that made it hard to focus. I'd have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours when I felt okay, then nothing for days when the fear and worry took over. I relied heavily on visual materials—diagrams, charts, videos—because they helped me absorb information quickly during my good moments. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me find some stability, but my routine was never what you'd call organized.
Q2: My pattern was completely dictated by my mental state and deadlines. Some weeks I couldn't bring myself to open anything because the anxiety was paralyzing, and then I'd have to cram everything in before a deadline. When I was scared about failing or not having enough money for next semester, studying felt impossible. But when a deadline loomed, that fear transformed into motivation, and I'd work intensely. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I survived.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in—either realizing a deadline was approaching or suddenly remembering I hadn't checked announcements in days. Sometimes it was after a mindfulness session when I felt calmer and more capable of facing my coursework. The fear of missing something important was a constant driver. I'd also log in when I needed to download materials to study offline since I couldn't always rely on stable internet access.
Q4: Not really, no. I never developed a comfortable routine with the LMS because logging in often triggered my anxiety about everything I was behind on. The closest I got was checking discussion posts when I felt socially connected, which helped with my mood. But calling it a 'normal routine' would be generous—it was more like sporadic bursts of activity when I could manage my fear enough to engage.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm a visual learner and could download them to watch offline. I'd pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual explanations when I was in a good headspace. Discussion posts were surprisingly helpful too—interacting with peers made me feel less alone in my struggles. Those twenty peer interactions I had were genuinely meaningful to me and helped combat the isolation I felt.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials, especially long text-heavy PDFs, because they didn't match my learning style and felt overwhelming when I was anxious. Some of the optional supplementary resources just sat there untouched because I had to prioritize what was essential for assignments. When you're scared and stretched thin, you can't engage with everything—you triage what will help you survive.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate, practical results—like understanding a concept I needed for an assignment or exam. Visual content that I could process quickly and retain was invaluable. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, I couldn't justify spending my limited energy on it. When you're running on anxiety and limited resources, every minute has to count.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones triggered a fear-driven urgency that actually helped me complete them, even when I was struggling. Optional things, no matter how interesting, got pushed aside because I was in survival mode. There's a strange irony there—the pressure of requirements actually helped me function, while freedom to explore felt paralyzing.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download videos and materials during my brief LMS sessions, then study them extensively on my own. I created visual notes, diagrams, and mind maps that helped me process information in my own way. I also used free external resources—YouTube explanations, open textbooks—because I couldn't afford supplementary materials. The LMS was just my gateway to content, not where learning actually happened.
Q10: So many situations. I'd watch downloaded lecture videos multiple times while taking detailed visual notes, but the LMS only registered one view. I'd spend hours creating color-coded diagrams and flowcharts from the material, completely offline. My best study sessions happened at odd hours when I couldn't sleep due to anxiety, working from printed notes or downloaded files. The depth of my engagement was invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: Constantly. My internet access isn't always reliable, and I learned early to download everything I might need. I'd access a video once, download it, and then watch it three or four more times while studying. Same with lecture slides—I'd print them when I could access a library printer and annotate them extensively by hand. One download often represented hours of subsequent offline study.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety was so overwhelming that even thinking about coursework made me feel physically ill. Financial stress would spike—worrying about rent, food, whether I could even continue studying—and everything academic felt impossible. The fear would become paralyzing rather than motivating. During those times, my attendance dropped because I couldn't face being around people while feeling so scared and vulnerable.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from anxiety or overwhelmed by money worries, the LMS felt like another source of stress rather than a tool. I'd avoid logging in because seeing all my incomplete tasks would trigger panic. Competing responsibilities—like working to afford basics—directly cut into my study time. Sometimes I was so fatigued from stress that I'd stare at the screen without absorbing anything.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be racing with fears about the future. I'd scroll through materials while actually planning how to stretch my budget for the week. Those moments of physical presence but mental absence were frustrating because I knew I was wasting precious time, but I couldn't force my anxious brain to focus.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts sometimes because social interaction felt too vulnerable when I was already feeling scared and fragile. I'd avoid starting big assignments because the fear of not doing well enough was paralyzing—classic anxiety-driven procrastination. Even knowing something was important didn't override the fear response. The mindfulness practice helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was incredibly difficult.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense spikes of anxiety that were unpleasant but ultimately productive. I'd cram most of my LMS activity into the days before deadlines because that's when the fear of failure finally outweighed the fear of engaging. My login patterns probably looked erratic, but those bursts were when I did my most focused work. Without deadlines, I might have been paralyzed indefinitely.
Q17: Positive feedback was incredibly important for my fragile confidence. When I received good scores on assignments, it gave me a brief window of reduced anxiety where I could engage more freely. Negative or even neutral feedback could send me spiraling, making it harder to approach the next task. I learned to time my feedback-checking for when I'd done my mindfulness exercises and felt more resilient.
Q18: The course structure was actually helpful because it gave me clear expectations I could work toward during my functional periods. Having defined deadlines meant I could plan my crisis-mode study sessions. However, the assumption that students would engage regularly didn't account for people like me who work in intense bursts. The flexibility to submit assignments helped accommodate my unpredictable capacity.
Q19: The video content being downloadable was crucial—whoever designed that understood students might need offline access. The discussion forums were welcoming, which helped me feel connected despite my irregular attendance. What made engagement harder was any real-time requirement, since my good periods didn't always align with scheduled activities. Also, having too many small tasks felt overwhelming when I was already anxious about bigger assignments.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in my worst moments—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but being too anxious or overwhelmed to actually produce work. They might be gathering materials for offline study that doesn't show in the system. Or they could be paralyzed by perfectionism, researching extensively but unable to start writing. High activity doesn't mean productive activity when you're struggling mentally.
Q21: That's literally my experience. I barely logged in, but I achieved a distinction because my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials quickly, then spend hours with them away from the LMS. My exam and assignment scores reflect deep engagement that the login data completely misses. Low LMS activity can mask intensive, effective studying that just doesn't happen on the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when students access materials and whether they meet deadlines. They might indicate students who are completely disengaged versus those who at least check in. But they miss everything that happens offline—the hours of studying downloaded materials, the handwritten notes, the genuine understanding developed away from the screen. They especially miss the emotional and psychological dimensions of engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when my anxious brain quiets enough that I can actually absorb and think critically about what I'm learning. For me, engagement often looks like intense offline sessions where I'm creating visual summaries and really wrestling with concepts. It's also feeling connected enough to participate in discussions and learn from peers, even when that's scary.
Q24: The LMS would never show the mindfulness sessions that made my engagement possible, or the hours I spent managing anxiety so I could study. It wouldn't capture the financial stress that competed for my mental energy, or how scared I felt most of the time. It couldn't show how much courage it took just to log in some days, or how meaningful those peer interactions were for my wellbeing. My distinction grade emerged from invisible struggle and resilience that no data system could track.
